coredata.dc:title: Study in a novel lithium-ion semi-solid-state batteries: Specific equivalent circuit modeling and improved SOC estimation method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chao
Wu, Chao
Yi, Jingguang
Su, Xiaoli
Cao, Xiangyang
Zhang, Kaixin
coredata.dc:description: Semi-solid-state batteries (SSSBs) combine the high safety and energy density of solid-state batteries with the superior conductivity and longevity of liquid-state batteries. This paper presents a novel SSSB and introduces a targeted modeling and state of charge (SOC) estimation method for its optimization. Initially, by analyzing the internal structure and interfacial properties, the SSSB’s advantages over liquid-state and all-solid-state batteries are highlighted. Subsequently, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and offline parameter identification techniques are employed to construct a specific equivalent circuit model (ECM) that incorporates the unique interfacial properties of the SSSB. This step ensures that the model accurately reflects the battery’s behavior under various conditions. Building on this foundation, an improved SOC correction method for end-of-discharge SOC correction is proposed. Based on the SOC estimation results from the extended Kalman filter and the backpropagation neural network (BPNN), a second-layer BPNN is further employed for model training. It trains on discharge data prior to the deviation of EKF’s end-of-discharge SOC estimation and corrects the terminal SOC, ensuring cost-effectiveness and timeliness. Finally, experimental validation demonstrates the effectiveness of this method in enhancing the accuracy and robustness of end-of-discharge SOC estimation for the SSSB. This work contributes to the development and application of lithium-ion SSSBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Semi-solid-state battery
Equivalent circuit model
State of charge
Extended Kalman filter
Backpropagation neural network

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Internal structure and interfacial properties of semi-solid-state batteries
Equivalent circuit model of semi-solid batteries
Equivalent circuit model analysis by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
State of charge (SOC) estimation and improvement
SOC estimation by extended Kalman filter (EKF)
SOC estimation by backpropagation neural network (BPNN)
Correcting end SOC estimation by multi-modal information interactive fusion (MMI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of lithium-ion semi-solid-state battery (SSSB).

Battery internal structure analysis: (a) schematic diagram of the NCM-21700 internal structure, (b) schematic diagram of the ASSB internal structure, and (c) schematic diagram of the novel SSSB internal structure.

Equivalent circuit model (ECM) structure analysis: (a) comparison of EIS curves with the NCM-21700 and lithium-ion SSSB, and (b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curve fitting and ECM components corresponding to the internal mechanism by ZView.

Experimental results for the parameterization offline with hybrid pulse power characteristic (HPPC): (a) graphical representation of HPPC method and (b) model accuracy verification by the SSSB terminal voltage under the simulated conditions (UDDS).

Results of online identification of model parameters and SOC estimation: (a) terminal voltage and error under UDDS conditions and (b) SOC estimation under UDDS conditions by the extended Kalman filter (EKF), and deviation in the discharge end area.
State of charge (SOC) estimation by backpropagation neural network (BPNN): the end of discharge SOC estimation result by BPNN.
Schematic diagram of multi-modal information interactive fusion (MMI-corrected) method.

SOC estimation results and errors of MMI-corrected algorithm: (a)The performance metrics of MMI under UDDS - mean squared error,(b)The performance metrics of MMI under UDDS- regression coefficients, (c) SOC contrast under UDDS, and (d) error contrast under UDDS (e) SOC contrast under DST, and (f) error contrast under DST.
Comparison of different SOC estimation methods under UDDS.
Comparison of different SOC estimation methods under DST.

head.ce:title: Study in a novel lithium-ion semi-solid-state batteries: Specific equivalent circuit modeling and improved SOC estimation method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai, 200082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering, Shanghai University of Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=School of Electrical Engineering,Shanghai University of Electric Power, city=Shanghai, postcode=200082, country=China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Luoyang Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Luoyang Institute of Science and Technology, city=Luoyang, postcode=471000, country=China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Luoyang Institute of Science and Technology, Luoyang, 471000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Luoyang Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Semi-solid-state batteries (SSSBs) combine the high safety and energy density of solid-state batteries with the superior conductivity and longevity of liquid-state batteries. This paper presents a novel SSSB and introduces a targeted modeling and state of charge (SOC) estimation method for its optimization. Initially, by analyzing the internal structure and interfacial properties, the SSSB’s advantages over liquid-state and all-solid-state batteries are highlighted. Subsequently, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and offline parameter identification techniques are employed to construct a specific equivalent circuit model (ECM) that incorporates the unique interfacial properties of the SSSB. This step ensures that the model accurately reflects the battery’s behavior under various conditions. Building on this foundation, an improved SOC correction method for end-of-discharge SOC correction is proposed. Based on the SOC estimation results from the extended Kalman filter and the backpropagation neural network (BPNN), a second-layer BPNN is further employed for model training. It trains on discharge data prior to the deviation of EKF’s end-of-discharge SOC estimation and corrects the terminal SOC, ensuring cost-effectiveness and timeliness. Finally, experimental validation demonstrates the effectiveness of this method in enhancing the accuracy and robustness of end-of-discharge SOC estimation for the SSSB. This work contributes to the development and application of lithium-ion SSSBs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the increasing emphasis on environmental protection and renewable energy, electric vehicles have experienced substantial development. To facilitate the complete shift from internal combustion engine vehicles to electric transportation, lithium-ion batteries have been widely adopted for their high energy densities, long cycle lives, and excellent low-temperature performances [1]. Traditional lithium-ion batteries primarily use liquid electrolytes to facilitate the transfer of lithium ions between the cathode and anode. In certain extreme cases, the separators in liquid lithium-ion batteries are susceptible to penetration by lithium dendrites, which can potentially cause internal short circuits and increase the risk of fire or explosion. In light of these issues, solid-state battery technology has emerged and rapidly become a focal point of research in the battery and energy storage fields. Compared to traditional liquid lithium-ion batteries, solid-state batteries offer greater safety and energy densities [2,3]. Solid-state battery technology can be categorized into all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) and semi-solid-state batteries (SSSBs), each featuring a variety of manufacturing techniques and displaying distinct technological characteristics and application potential.

To further advance the development and application of solid-state batteries, it is crucial to establish an accurate model to describe and predict the battery performance and behavior. Commonly used battery modeling techniques include empirical models, mechanistic models, equivalent circuit models (ECM), and coupled models [4,5]. Empirical models are primarily based on the analysis of extensive battery data. These models use data correlations and common battery behavior characteristics to describe and predict battery status through regression models or empirical formulas [6].

Mechanistic models are based on the electrochemical processes of batteries, utilizing reaction kinetics and porous electrode theory. These models include the P2D model, the single-particle model, and a range of simplified electrochemical models or extended single-particle models [7–9]. For example, Guo et al. [10] developed a dual-layered LiF/LiBO-Ag interphase that enables dendrite-free Li deposition at high current densities, enhancing cycling stability and energy density for practical quasi-solid-state high-energy-density lithium metal batteries. To enhance the model accuracy, particularly at high current densities, Kazemi et al. [11] implemented a calibration strategy for diffusion coefficients related to the lithium-ion concentration. This approach improved the model’s predictive accuracy, ensuring its effectiveness even at current densities as high as
5mA /
cm
2
. Raijmakers et al. [12] developed an advanced model by integrating the double-layer capacitance at the electrode and electrolyte interfaces, considering the ion migration kinetics within the cathode and the geometric capacitance between the current collectors. However, mechanistic models are challenging to implement in battery management systems (BMSs) for solid-state batteries, necessitating more simplified models. The equivalent circuit model (ECM), with its simplicity in modeling, ease of parameter identification, high accuracy, suitability for mathematical analysis, and broad applicability, is widely used.

The ECM analyzes the external voltage and current characteristics of batteries by simulating the physical and chemical reactions within them. This model uses various circuit components to emulate the battery’s electrical behavior and typically relies on time-domain measurements (TDM) or frequency-domain measurements (FDM) to fit its voltage response [13]. This circuit analysis technique effectively establishes mathematical models to describe the dynamic responses and degradation behaviors of batteries. In the field of traditional liquid electrolyte lithium-ion batteries, ECM techniques are well-developed, with researchers making specific optimizations for different applications. Tran et al. [14] considered the impacts of the battery state of health (SOH), state of charge (SOC), and temperature on the ECM, proposing an empirical model that integrates these effects to enhance the accuracy and reliability of the ECM. Liu et al. [15] proposed an autoregressive-based equivalent circuit model (AR-ECM), taking into account the interrelationships between the SOH, SOC, and state of power (SOP). Experimental results demonstrated that the AR-ECM provides high accuracy and robustness in estimating the SOC and SOH. Li et al. [16] developed a fractional-order equivalent circuit model for lithium-ion batteries based on a second-order resistance–capacitance (RC) equivalent circuit model. Simulation results indicated that under various cycling test conditions, the model achieved a root mean square error (RMSE) of less than 9 mV and a mean relative error of no less than 0.1%, demonstrating excellent accuracy and robustness. Zheng et al. [17] developed an electrochemistry-based equivalent circuit model (E-ECM) with a decoupling parameters extraction (DPE) method, improving voltage response simulation, especially in low SOC regions, and accurately describing battery performance under dynamic and constant current conditions. Xu et al. [18] proposed an improved equivalent circuit model (IECM) incorporating electrochemical mechanisms. This model integrates a calculation method for the open-circuit voltage (OCV) that considers solid-phase diffusion processes in traditional ECMs. Subsequent joint state estimation of lithium-ion batteries using the IECM demonstrated its enhanced accuracy and robustness.

Coupled models, which are more complex, integrate various modeling techniques to provide a comprehensive analysis of batteries. These generally include thermal–electric coupled models, electrochemical–thermal–mechanical coupled models, and electrochemical–thermal–aging effect coupled models [19–21].

While solid-state battery fabrication techniques have made some progress, modeling studies are still in their initial stages, with insufficient findings. Therefore, developing an ECM suitable for both ASSBs and SSSBs is crucial to facilitate the development of solid-state battery technology. This study focused on modeling the electrical characteristics of semi-solid-state batteries, aiming to construct a model that accurately depicts the internal electrochemical processes and interfacial properties, and the voltage–current responses in practical applications were effectively simulated.

In this study, an ECM was developed for semi-solid batteries, integrating electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and parameter identification techniques to effectively simulate the electrical characteristics and accurately estimate the SOC. The main content and contributions of this paper are as follows. Section 1 reviews the modeling methods for lithium-ion batteries and highlights the insufficient research on the electrical characteristic modeling of ASSBs and SSSBs, indicating a need for further exploration. Section 2 thoroughly analyzes the internal mechanisms of the SSSB proposed in this paper. The structure and composition of the battery are described in detail, and the charge transfer mechanism and the interface properties are explored, revealing the barriers to lithium-ion transport in the semi-solid-state battery. Section 3 establishes a specific second-order RC ECM (RC-ECM) for the lithium-ion SSSB. Based on the internal mechanism, the electrical components corresponding to the internal losses were analyzed using EIS technology. Then, the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm was used to determine the ECM parameters under the hybrid pulse power characteristic (HPPC) conditions. The results showed that the second-order RC-ECM has good accuracy. However, it is less accurate at the end of discharge. Section 4 proposes a novel method that addresses the limitations of the second-order RC-ECM in the SOC estimation accuracy by integrating the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and backpropagation neural network (BPNN) techniques, thereby significantly enhancing the SOC estimation accuracy at the end of discharge. Section 5 provides the conclusions and outlook, highlighting the limitations of this work and suggesting directions for future research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal structure and interfacial properties of semi-solid-state batteries
ce:section.ce:para:
This paper adopts a lithium-ion semi-solid-state battery, which was developed by our research group in collaboration with the enterprise. The cathode consisted of a nickel-rich layered oxide (
Li
Ni
0 . 9
Co
0 . 05
Mn
0 . 05
O
2
,NCM), the anode was a carbon–silicon composite, and the electrolyte was a conventional organic liquid electrolyte. In particular, the separator of this lithium-ion SSSB has been improved, consisting of a base separator and a typical garnet-type oxide solid electrolyte
Li
7
La
3
Zr
2
O
12
(LLZO). The schematic diagram of the lithium-ion SSSB is shown in Fig. 1.

The cathode material was a nickel-rich layered oxide,
Li
Ni
0 . 9
Co
0 . 05
Mn
0 . 05
O
2
. Generally, nickel-rich NCM provides a higher capacity but results in a poorer cycle life and thermal stability. The similar ionic radii of
Ni
2 +
and
Li
+
, the ease of phase transitions and poor thermal stability of nickel-rich NCM material negatively affects the battery performance, cycle life, and safety [22]. The high nickel content in the cathode material enhances the energy density due to the increased lithium-ion storage capacity, but it also leads to challenges such as structural instability and side reactions during cycling, which can cause capacity fading and safety concerns. the cathode material of semi-solid battery in this paper had a nickel content as high as 0.9. We addressed the issues of lifespan and safety associated with the nickel-rich NCM cathode material through the structural design of the semi-solid-state battery.

The anode material was a carbon–silicon composite with minimal silicon content. Graphite has high structural stability, which provided a better cycle life and ease of manufacturing. Adding a small amount of silicon could significantly increase the energy density of the battery, matching the high-nickel content cathode material. The silicon in the anode material can store more lithium ions due to its high capacity, but it also experiences significant volume expansion during lithiation, which can lead to structural degradation. The carbon matrix in the composite helps to buffer this expansion and maintain the structural integrity of the anode.

The semi-solid-state battery in this paper comprised both liquid and solid-state components. The liquid component was the liquid electrolyte, while the solid-state component was the separator, which consisted of a base separator (PP/PE) and a garnet-type oxide (LLZO). The garnet-type oxide solid electrolyte LLZO has excellent chemical stability, a high lithium-ion conductivity, and shear modulus, making it one of the most promising solid electrolyte materials. LLZO’s high lithium-ion conductivity is attributed to its unique crystal structure, which allows for fast lithium-ion transport pathways. Its chemical stability also prevents it from reacting with the cathode material, reducing the risk of side reactions and improving battery lifespan. However, using LLZO alone in ASSBs as the electrolyte still presents some issues. First, due to the strong alkalinity of the LLZO surface, it easily reacts with
H
2
O
and
CO
2
in the air to form a
Li
2
CO
3
film. Since
Li
2
CO
3
has a very low lithium-ion conductivity, it leads to a decrease in the lithium-ion transport rate and an increase in the interfacial impedance [23,24]; Second, due to its extremely high hardness and shear modulus, the contact between the LLZO solid electrolyte and the electrode materials becomes “hard contact”. This leads to an increased interfacial impedance, non-uniform electrochemical reactions, and accumulation of interfacial stress. The hard contact between LLZO and the electrode materials can also cause mechanical stress, which may lead to cracks or delamination at the interface, further degrading the battery performance. Therefore, LLZO was used as part of the battery separator in the SSSB, it combined the advantages of ASSBs and liquid lithium-ion batteries. Fig. 2 shows the internal structure of the liquid lithium-ion battery (NCM-21700), the ASSBs and the novel lithium-ion SSSB. the lithium-ion SSSB in this paper reduced the composition of liquid electrolyte compared to traditional liquid lithium-ion batteries, reducing the safety risk of liquid leakage. And the design of LLZO as part of the separator reduced the risk of lithium dendrites penetrating through the separator, greatly avoiding internal short circuits. Meanwhile, it solved the interface problem of LLZO in ASSBs by applying liquid electrolyte coating on both sides of the separator (base separator and LLZO).

LLZO materials face some key interfacial issues in the application of ASSBs, such as the interfacial contact, charge transfer, and electrochemical stability at the cathode, and the growth of lithium dendrites at the anode interface [25]. These interfacial issues can significantly affect the overall performance and safety of the battery. For instance, poor interfacial contact can lead to increased resistance and reduced efficiency, while the growth of lithium dendrites can cause internal short circuits and potential thermal runaway.

Regarding the growth of lithium dendrites, several studies have indicated that the mechanism of lithium metal penetration in LLZO electrolytes differs significantly from the growth of lithium dendrites in liquid electrolytes. The primary factors contributing to this difference include structural defects in the solid electrolyte, interfacial stress, and non-uniform interfacial contact [26–28]. Several studies have proposed methods, such as chemical etching, high-temperature treatment, and mechanical polishing, to remove the
Li
2
CO
3
film on the LLZO surface to address the issues at the solid electrolyte–anode interface. These methods improve the interfacial contact between the LLZO and the anode, reduce interfacial impedance, and increase the critical current density. However, these approaches often increase the surface roughness and defects of the LLZO, hindering uniform lithium deposition at the interface. The increased surface roughness and defects can lead to uneven lithium deposition, which can further exacerbate the growth of lithium dendrites and compromise the battery’s performance and safety. Therefore, a SSSB with liquid electrolyte and LLZO as part of the separator material were adopted in this study, effectively solving these issues. And the SSSB has an internal interface different from that of ASSBs. The unique internal interface of the SSSB allows for better compatibility between the liquid electrolyte and the solid electrolyte, reducing interfacial resistance and improving overall battery performance.

Consequently, the properties of the electrolyte–anode interface were consistent with those of traditional liquid lithium–ion batteries. The electrochemical stability window of traditional liquid organic electrolytes is approximately 1–4.5 V, whereas the operating voltage of a graphite anode is about 0.05 V, exceeding the stability window of ordinary electrolytes. Therefore, the graphite anodes in lithium-ion batteries are theoretically unstable. During charge and discharge, lithium ions are consumed as the electrolyte decomposes, forming a passivation layer on the graphite anode surface known as the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film [29]. This results in a rapid drop in battery capacity during the pre-cycle period of the battery. In the SSSB discussed in this paper, a layer of liquid electrolyte was introduced between the cathode and the separator. During the initial cycles, a passivation layer, known as the cathode–electrolyte interface (CEI) film, formed on the cathode surface. The CEI film was typically thinner than the SEI film and did not cover the entire cathode surface. However, it grew with the number of cycles. For the interface between the liquid electrolyte coating and the solid electrolyte (LLZO) separator, recent research showed that the electrochemical stability window of solid electrolyte LLZO is approximately 0.05–2.91 V [30]. In contrast, the electrochemical stability window of traditional liquid electrolytes is about 1–4.5 V. Both fall within a similar range, indicating good electrochemical stability and no additional electrochemical side reaction products.

In summary, during the charge–discharge cycles of SSSBs, the resistance to lithium-ion transport includes interfacial resistance between the cathode and the liquid electrolyte, transport resistance between the liquid electrolyte and the solid electrolyte separator, and solid-phase diffusion resistance within the cathode and anode particles. These resistances collectively determine the overall lithium-ion transport efficiency in the battery. By optimizing the interfacial contact and reducing these resistances, the performance and lifespan of the SSSB can be significantly improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equivalent circuit model of semi-solid batteries

ce:section.ce:para: This study used SSSBs and NCM-21700 cylindrical lithium-ion batteries with conventional liquid electrolytes. The experimental setup included a host computer, a high-performance battery testing system (NEWARE-CT-4008-5V40A-NA), and a multi-channel electrochemical workstation (Princeton-PARSTAT MC). A schematic diagram of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 3

The specific experimental procedure for the SSSB was as follows. First, five charge/discharge cycles were performed to activate the battery performance. The battery was charged at a constant current rate of 0.25C until the cutoff voltage was 4.25 V, and then it was charged at a constant voltage until the current rate dropped to 0.02C. Similarly, the battery was discharged at a constant current rate of 0.25C until a cutoff voltage of 2.5 V was reached. Next, a capacity test was performed with a standard charge/discharge cycle of 0.5C to verify that the battery cycling conditions were stable. Finally, reference performance tests (RPTs) were performed, including EIS tests, HPPC tests, and undischarged discharge under simulated conditions (UDDS) tests and Dynamic Stress Test (DST) tests. The EIS test was conducted after the battery was fully charged and left to rest for 5 h to stabilize the internal environment. Testing was performed once the battery was in equilibrium [31]. The EIS test in this study used a potentiostatic mode (PEIS) with frequencies set from 0.01 to 10,000 Hz and a voltage amplitude of 10 mV. The HPPC test involved fully charging the battery, allowing it to rest for 2 h, then discharging at a constant current of 0.5C to a 10% SOC, and allowing it to rest again for 2 h. This process was repeated until the battery was fully discharged. A comparison EIS test was conducted using an NCM-21700 lithium battery with the same cathode and anode materials as the SSSB, following the same experimental procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equivalent circuit model analysis by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: EIS is a crucial non-destructive measurement method for studying electrochemical interface processes. It allows for a detailed understanding of the electrochemical reactions of a battery over a wide frequency range, providing theoretical electrochemical foundations for establishing an ECM and determining the appropriate components of the ECM. The principle of EIS testing involves applying a sinusoidal voltage of a certain amplitude and frequency and measuring the amplitude and phase shift of the output voltage or current. This process is repeated over various frequencies, typically ranging from kHz to mHz, generating an electrochemical impedance spectrum. The impedance spectrum is usually represented using a Nyquist plot, where the real part of the impedance (Z) is plotted on the x -axis and the negative imaginary part of the impedance is typically plotted on the y -axis. This representation places the capacitive elements of the battery impedance in the first quadrant of the Nyquist plot. This method allows the detection of electrochemical processes within the battery that occur over different time constants.

The Nyquist plots obtained from the EIS tests of the SSSB and NCM-21700 battery are shown in Fig. 4(a), which had similar shapes and compositions. It should be noted that in the ultra-high-frequency range, the impedance spectrum was affected by the inductive behavior caused by the wires connecting the battery and the measuring equipment or the battery’s geometry [32,33]. Therefore, this study ignored the inductive effects in the ultra-high-frequency part of the EIS curve. The EIS curve can be divided into three parts: a high-frequency region, mid-frequency region, and low-frequency region. The high-frequency region intersected the real axis, representing the sum of the ohmic resistance components within the battery, including the solid electrolyte, liquid electrolyte coating, active material, and current collector [34], with the electrolyte resistance being dominant. The semicircle in the mid-frequency region represents the movement of lithium ions within the SEI film between the electrolyte and the electrode as well as the charge transfer process of the electrochemical reaction process. The presence of only one semicircle in the mid-frequency region indicates similar time constants for the electrochemical processes occurring on the electrode, leading to the merging of semicircles on the EIS plot [35]. Generally, the low-frequency region represents the diffusion process of lithium ions in the active material (solid phase) of the electrode.

Based on the analysis of the internal interface properties of SSSBs, the EIS curves reflected the ohmic resistance of the internal components (ohmic loss), lithium-ion transport impedance at the electrode–electrolyte interface (interface loss), and lithium-ion solid-phase diffusion impedance (diffusion loss). In terms of diffusion processes, compared with conventional liquid electrolyte Li-ion batteries, SSSBs contain LLZO at the separator, and the working mechanism of the solid-state electrolytes (LLZO) mainly relies on Li-ion diffusion as the driving force [36]. Thus, in this study, we considered the solid-phase diffusion impedance of the SSSBs to also include Li-ion diffusion inside the LLZO at the separator. Compared to the EIS curve of the NCM-21700 battery, the curve of the SSSB was shifted in the positive x -direction, the semicircle region was larger, and the slope in the low-frequency region was greater. The shift indicated an increase in the ohmic resistance. A larger semicircle suggested more severe interface loss, and a larger low-frequency slope was associated with a greater diffusion capacity, verifying the diffusion process of lithium ions in the LLZO. Additionally, since LLZO has an electrochemical window comparable to that of the above discussed electrolytes and exhibits good electrochemical stability without generating additional reaction products, the SSSB did not generate additional interfaces. And the presence of liquid electrolyte prevented the formation of a
Li
2
CO
3
film on the surface of the LLZO, it avoided generating an additional interface about the
Li
2
CO
3
film. Therefore, no additional semicircle was created on the EIS curve. The SSSB had a similar EIS profile to the NCM-21700.

ZView was used to fit the EIS curve of the SSSB, and the fitting results and ECM structure are shown in Fig. 4(b). The electrochemical effects in the high-frequency and mid-frequency regions correspond to the same circuit structure as that in conventional liquid-state batteries. The solid-phase diffusion process within the active materials of the positive and negative electrodes and the LLZO in the separator in the low-frequency region was also represented by an RC parallel circuit. This ensured that the model did not overlook the solid-phase diffusion process within the SSSB, providing higher accuracy compared to that of the traditional first-order RC-ECM that uses Warburg impedance to simulate the diffusion process (which neglects Warburg impedance).

In studies of conventional liquid lithium-ion batteries, equivalent circuit models (ECMs) typically utilize first-order RC-ECM and second-order RC-ECM [37]. These models simplify the battery’s electrochemical behavior, allowing simulation of its dynamic characteristics using circuit elements. Each RC parallel circuit in these models has a distinct physical meaning, corresponding to different electrochemical processes and physical phenomena within the battery, thus enabling more accurate description of the battery’s charge and discharge behavior and internal state [38,39]. In conclusion, the difference of ECM between the SSSB and conventional liquid-state batteries lies in the representation of the solid-phase diffusion process in the low-frequency region. Although the ECM parameters could be directly extracted through EIS, high-precision sensors were required to measure the voltage and current due to the injection of small signals, making high-precision real-time EIS measurement a challenge. Additionally, the cost of the experimental equipment raises the requirements for EIS measurement technology. Compared to EIS, the measurement conditions of HPPC are more convenient [40].

As previously mentioned, the HPPC experiment was conducted on the SSSB, resulting in the voltage curve shown in Fig. 5(a). After discharging by a 10% SOC, the system was left to rest, resulting in an instantaneous voltage response. This phenomenon represented the ohmic resistance effect. Ohmic resistance can be calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
R
0
=
|
Δ
U
b a t
I
|
.

Where the I represented the current, and
Δ
U
bat
represented the voltage drop. The subsequent two-hour rest period aimed to allow the battery voltage to approach a fully relaxed state. At this point, the current was zero, and the voltage at the SSSB terminal could be calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
U

.

The OCV value represents the terminal voltage after a two-hour rest period.
R
1
denotes the SEI film resistance, and
R
2
denotes the charge transfer resistance.
U

τ

, and
τ

1
are unknowns. By letting
a =
U

τ

( 0 )
,
d = 1 /
τ
2
, Eq. (2) can be simplified to by Eq. (3).
(3)
f

e
− b t

e
− d t
.

The parameters were determined using the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm. PSO has advantages such as a strong adaptability, fast convergence, and simplicity in implementation, making it an ideal identification algorithm for the second-order RC-ECM [41]. The objective function is defined by Eq. (4).
(4)
ɛ
t
= m i n i m i z e
∑
t ∈ 2 h
u
t
− f
t
2
.

After iterative fitting, the globally optimal fitting function was obtained. The ECM parameters were calculated by Eq. (5), Eq. (6), Eq. (7), Eq. (8) based on the values of a , b , c , and d , as follows:
(5)
R
1
=
a
1 −
e
− b t
I
,
(6)
C
1
=
1
b
R
1
,
(7)
R
2
=
c
1 −
e
− d t
I
,
(8)
C
2
=
1
d
R
1
.

By using this method, the parameters of the second-order RC-ECM were obtained. To further verify the accuracy and validity of the model and parameter identification, interpolation was performed on the ECM parameters in discrete states. The SSSB was then discharged under dynamic conditions (UDDS), and the simulation results of the proposed ECM were compared with the experimental results, as shown in Fig. 5(b). It can be seen that the simulated terminal voltage of the proposed ECM closely matched the actual terminal voltage. Additionally, the calculated ECM evaluation metrics are shown in Table 1, demonstrating the high accuracy of this ECM. However, the evaluation metrics were lower than those obtained using the second-order RC-ECM for conventional liquid lithium-ion batteries, indicating that the traditional second-order RC-ECM is not the optimal electrical characteristic model for SSSBs. This may affect subsequent applications of SSSBs (e.g., SOC estimation).

ce:section.ce:section-title: State of charge (SOC) estimation and improvement
ce:section.ce:para: The State of Charge (SOC) estimation techniques for conventional liquid lithium-ion batteries have approached maturity. Meng et al. [42] presented a framework combining aging mechanism analysis and machine learning to estimate LiFePO4 battery capacity under slight overcharge. and they [43] presented a state of charge estimation framework for LiFePO4 batteries that integrates physics calculation, adaptive Kalman filter, and machine learning, achieving strong robustness and higher accuracy with a maximum relative error below 2%. To further advance the development and application of SSSBs, we used the ECM described earlier to estimate the SOC under UDDS conditions. Given the inherent deficiencies of the second-order RC-ECM, this paper proposes a method of multimodal information interaction and fusion (combining model-driven and data-driven approaches) for SOC estimation to address the accuracy issues of the second-order RC-ECM in the discharge terminal region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOC estimation by extended Kalman filter (EKF)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the proposed second-order RC-ECM, the EKF algorithm was used to estimate the SOC for the SSSB. First, the forgetting factor recursive least squares (FFRLS) method was used for online parameter identification of the ECM. After obtaining the parameters, the terminal voltage output of the ECM was calculated, and the error between the model terminal voltage and the actual terminal voltage was considered as the model error. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the model error was significant at the end of discharge, indicating the inherent deficiencies of the second-order RC-ECM for the SSSB. This led to poor model accuracy at the end of the discharge. Then, the EKF method was used for SOC estimation. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the overall SOC estimation results matched the actual SOC closely, effectively tracking the changes in the actual charge. However, there was a significant deviation between the SOC estimation and the actual SOC at the end of discharge.

In summary, due to the accuracy issues of the established ECM at the end of discharge, the EKF estimation of the SOC also produced significant errors at the terminal. A data-driven approach was adopted to correct the SOC estimation curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOC estimation by backpropagation neural network (BPNN)
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, battery data under UDDS discharge conditions was used, and a BPNN was employed to predict the last 20% of the SOC. The voltage, current, and actual SOC were selected as inputs, with the SOC as the output to train the neural network. The structure of the BPNN included an input layer, an output layer, and two hidden layers. The first hidden layer contained five neurons, and the second hidden layer contained 20 neurons.

The SOC estimation results are shown in Fig. 7. It can be seen that the overall trend of the SOC estimated by the BPNN agreed with the actual SOC decline trend, but the accuracy was poor. This was because the BPNN required a large amount of data for SOC estimation, and this study used a limited amount of data. This study employed a combined model-based and data-driven approach, which reduced the data requirements for the BPNN in subsequent SOC estimations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correcting end SOC estimation by multi-modal information interactive fusion (MMI)
ce:section.ce:para: Due to inaccurate SOC estimation at the end of discharge using the EKF, a multi-modal information interactive fusion (MMI) method combining a BPNN and an EKF was employed for corrections. The MMI method worked as follows. First, under various operating conditions, the EKF is first employed to estimate the SSSB’s SOC in real time. However, due to limitations in model accuracy, the EKF exhibits a trend deviation near the end of discharge. To address this issue, the first-layer BPNN is simultaneously utilized to estimate the SOC during the end-of-discharge phase. The first-layer BPNN is trained using pre-deviation data from the EKF (ranging from 80% SOC to the pre-end of discharge) to learn the inherent variation trend of the end-of-discharge SOC. Although the absolute estimation accuracy of this layer is relatively low, its output demonstrates significantly superior trend characteristics compared to the EKF’s end-of-discharge estimates, thereby forming complementary information modalities.

And then, The high-precision estimation results from the EKF and the trend-corrected outputs from the first-layer BPNN are fed into a second-layer BPNN for feature-level fusion through multi-modal information interaction. As an optimization network, the second-layer BPNN incorporates dynamic estimates from both information models and actual end-of-discharge SOC data. Since the outputs of the EKF and BPNN represent system dynamic characteristics and trend features, respectively, the network employs an adaptive weight allocation mechanism. This mechanism preserves the high precision of the EKF while leveraging the BPNN’s trend correction capability to suppress cumulative errors at the terminal stage.

At last, The proposed hierarchical architecture effectively integrates model-driven and data-driven approaches. The first-layer BPNN requires minimal training data (focused on the pre-deviation segment) and rapidly converges due to the high correlation between inputs and outputs. The second-layer BPNN enhances generalization capability through dual-modal inputs. This method overcomes the limitations of single-model approaches through information fusion, providing a novel framework for battery state estimation under complex operating conditions. The implementation flowchart of the algorithm is illustrated in Fig. 8.

Figs. 9(a) and 9(b) show the performance metrics for the optimally trained MMI model. The mean squared error (MSE) in Fig. 9(a) represents the expected value of the difference between predicted and target outputs, with lower values indicating better performance. The R coefficient in Fig. 9(b) represents the correlation between predicted and target outputs. An R value closer to 1 indicates a stronger relationship between the predicted and actual data. Therefore, it indicates that using the MMI model to correct the end SOC achieved optimal results.

To validate our proposed method, the accuracy of the SOC estimation was systematically analyzed. By comparing the performances of the EKF and the modified multi-modal information (MMI-corrected) methods, we found that both methods were highly accurate, but the MMI-corrected method performed better in terms of the SOC estimation accuracy. In the UDDS experiment, when estimating the SOC using the EKF method, the mean absolute error (MAE) was 0.0071, the root mean square error (RMSE) was 0.0085, and the coefficient of determination (
R
2
) reached 0.9991. In contrast, the MMI-corrected method had an MAE of 0.0057, an RMSE of 0.0074, and a higher
R
2
of 0.9995. In the DST experiment, when estimating the SOC using the EKF method, the mean absolute error (MAE) was 0.0066, the root mean square error (RMSE) was 0.0081, and the coefficient of determination (
R
2
) reached 0.9993. In contrast, the MMI-corrected method had an MAE of 0.0048, an RMSE of 0.0055, and a higher
R
2
of 0.9997. As shown in Tables 2 and 3, this demonstrated the high precision and stability of the MMI-corrected method in estimating the SOC under dynamic conditions. Additionally, the comparison of the terminal SOC estimation and error analysis curves clearly revealed that the MMI-corrected method had lower error and better stability than the EKF method in terminal SOC estimation, as shown in Fig. 9(c), 9(d),9(e) and 9(f).

Compared with other traditional SOC estimation methods (such as open-circuit voltage method, Coulomb counting method, etc.), the MMI method shows higher accuracy and stronger robustness. The open-circuit voltage method is easily affected by temperature and battery state of health, resulting in a large deviation in the estimation results. However, the Coulomb counting rule has a cumulative error, which will lead to a large SOC estimation error after long-term use [44,45]. In contrast, the MMI method can obtain more accurate SOC estimation with less data support by combining the advantages of EKF and BPNN, especially at the end of discharge, overcoming the shortcomings of traditional methods in accuracy. In addition, compared with single-modality methods (such as model-based or data-driven methods), MMI methods can reduce the requirements of data-driven methods for data quantity and quality, as well as the requirements for model accuracy of model-based methods. At last, compared to the combination of other methods [46], the MMI proposed in this paper trains on discharge data prior to terminal SOC deviation and corrects the terminal SOC, ensuring cost-effectiveness and timeliness.
This further validated the advantages of the improved method in terms of accuracy and practicality, providing strong technical support for the future application of SSSBs in BMSs.
In this paper, we proposed a Li-ion SSSB and developed a corresponding ECM and SOC estimation method for it. The main contributions and conclusions of this study are summarized as follows:
First, we proposed a Li-ion SSSB that offers improved safety and higher energy density than conventional lithium-ion batteries, addressing the interface contact problem in all-solid-state batteries.

Second, a second-order RC equivalent circuit model with a parallel RC circuit was proposed to represent the solid-phase diffusion process in the low-frequency range of the SSSB. The model parameters were identified using the PSO algorithm, and the model was validated, demonstrating its reasonable accuracy and reliability.

Finally, This paper proposes a new method to correct the SOC estimation, by employing a multimodal information interaction and fusion (MMI) technique, which integrates the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and a backpropagation neural network. The MMI trains on discharge data prior to terminal SOC deviation and corrects the terminal SOC, ensuring cost-effectiveness and timeliness. Verification under UDDS and DST conditions demonstrated that the SOC estimation accuracy at the discharge end using the MMI correction method was significantly superior to that of the EKF method. The coefficient of determination (
R
2
) of SOC has reached 99.95% and 99.97%.

The primary limitation of this study was the absence of a fully compatible ECM to accurately represent the electrical behavior of the SSSB. Consequently, optimization efforts are currently limited to the application level to mitigate the deficiencies of the existing model. Fulture research should focus on developing a more accurate ECM.
The English abbreviations and their corresponding meanings in this article are shown in Table 4.

Chao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chao Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Jingguang Yi: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Xiaoli Su: Writing – review & editing. Xiangyang Cao: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Kaixin Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Chao Wu reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Xiangyang Cao reports financial support was provided by Scientific and Technological Research Project of Henan Province. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 2237, 9107]; and the Scientific and Technological Research Project of Henan Province, China [grant numbers 242102210203].
